name: Rionorese

ancestors.notes: Sanabrian Leonese adstrate: the Sanabria comarca of Zamora province is geographically and linguistically adjacent to Rionor. Shared features include full betacism and θ-merger.

Old Portuguese (Aviz-period) adstrate: as Portuguese consolidated as the state language, the Leonese-speaking communities of Miranda, Rionor, and Guadramil faced intensifying pressure. Portuguese orthographic conventions (lh, nh, ã) penetrated the writing tradition.

notes: Rionorese (mirandês de Rionor / Rionorês), the nearly-extinct Asturleonese variety spoken in Rio de Onor (PT) / Rihonor de Castilla (ES), a single village straddling the Portuguese-Spanish border in Trás-os-Montes. Fewer than 100 elderly speakers as of the early 2000s (Macias 2003). DIVERGENCES FROM PARENT (ast-PT-x-medieval): (1) FULL BETACISM: /v/ → /b/ unconditionally — the /v/~/b/ contrast preserved in Mirandese and medieval Leonese is lost. ⟨v⟩ and ⟨b⟩ both surface as /b/ (with intervocalic fricative allophone [β]). (2) DENTAL FRICATIVE MERGER: the medieval 4-way sibilant system (t͡s, d͡z, s̺, z̺) collapses to a single dental fricative /θ/. ⟨c/ç⟩ before front vowels, ⟨z⟩, and ⟨ç⟩ all map to /θ/ — parallel to Castilian rather than Leonese. (3) ⟨ch⟩ = /ʃ/ (not /tʃ/): the affricate from the medieval parent has shifted to a simple fricative, under Portuguese influence (PT ch=/ʃ/) or through independent lenition. Rionorese retains ⟨tch⟩ for /tʃ/. (4) VELAR FRICATIVE: ⟨g⟩ + front vowel = /x/, parallel to Spanish. (5) RHOTICS: word-initial ⟨r⟩ is a trill /r/; intervocalic ⟨r⟩ and ⟨r⟩ after stops (onset) are flaps /ɾ/; ⟨rr⟩ always /r/. (6) DIPHTHONGS: ⟨iê⟩ /je/ (stressed Leonese diphthong), ⟨uâ⟩ /wa/, ⟨uô⟩ /wɔ/ mark stressed vowels. (7) WORD-FINAL NASAL: word-final ⟨n/m⟩ nasalizes the preceding vowel and is silent (handled by digraph graphemes ⟨an⟩=ɐ̃, ⟨en⟩=ẽ etc., inherited from parent). Sources: Macias (2003) 'Rionorese: a nearly extinct Asturian dialect of Portugal'; Leite de Vasconcellos (1882, 1900); Ferreira (1999).

sources.author: Penny, R.
sources.title: A History of the Spanish Language
